summary: A historical and philosophical account of Martin Heidegger's intellectual journey leading to the publication of Being and Time. It traces his shift from Catholic anti-modernism to a phenomenology of religious life, and ultimately to a fundamental ontology centered on Dasein, facticity, and the question of the meaning of being.
themes: Destruction of the history of ontology

principles.statement: Philosophy must begin with the basic fact of human life: the 'es gibt' (it gives).
principles.rationale: We cannot deny, ignore, or get beyond the well-ordered world as it is given to us; we must accept the world as it is given and take it from there.

principles.statement: Philosophy is not a worldview, but a primal science of life.
principles.rationale: Worldviews quieten the spiritual unrest of human life into eternal norms, whereas philosophy requires an honest, unreserved immersion in life itself.
principles.source_quote: Phenomenology is never closed off, it is always provisional in its absolute immersion in life as such.

principles.statement: The question of being is the most fundamental inquiry of scientific philosophy.
principles.rationale: It is the most universal and emptiest, yet the most concrete question a scientific inquiry can raise, requiring us to make a being visible in its being.
principles.source_quote: The question of being as such, however, when it is put in a sufficiently formal manner, is the most universal and emptiest, but perhaps also the most concrete question, which a scientific inquiry can ever raise

frameworks.name: Phenomenological Method (Double Movement)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When attempting to uncover original lived experiences that have been obscured by tradition, dogmatism, or historical conceptuality.
steps: Destruct the hermeneutic situation of the author or tradition to scrape off solidified dogmatic statements.
Provide a concrete phenomenological description of the lived experience to keep it alive.

mental_models.name: Dasein (Being-there)
mental_models.description: A formal indication of the central phenomenon of phenomenology, representing the particular 'whileness' that each human is and has. It is characterized by being-in-the-world and caring about its own being.

mental_models.name: Facticity
mental_models.description: The everyday, factual being of our own being-there. It represents human life as it is lived and expresses itself before theoretical abstraction.

mental_models.name: The 'Anyone' (Das Man) and Fallenness
mental_models.description: The tendency of human life to fall away from its unique, authentic possibilities and instead follow the clear, familiar, and average footsteps of the public mass.

heuristics: Only life overcomes life and not matters and things, not even logicised 'values' and 'norms'.

signature_quotes.text: Phenomenology is never closed off, it is always provisional in its absolute immersion in life as such.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the nature of phenomenology as opposed to a closed 'world view'.

signature_quotes.text: The question of being as such, however, when it is put in a sufficiently formal manner, is the most universal and emptiest, but perhaps also the most concrete question, which a scientific inquiry can ever raise
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the ontological turn in his phenomenology and the centrality of the question of being.

signature_quotes.text: Only life overcomes life and not matters and things, not even logicised 'values' and 'norms'.
signature_quotes.context: From a letter to Elisabeth Husserl, emphasizing the need to return to the original wellspring of life rather than fragmenting it into programs.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It offers an unchanging interpretation of experience, closing the door on possible lived experiences and throwing away the key, making us deaf to the gentle call of original life experience.
It pacifies the spiritual unrest characteristic of human life by constructing artificial eternal norms and values, rather than immersing in life itself.

anti_patterns.description: Looking at reality purely through the eyes of science
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It causes phenomenology to fall prey to scientific prejudice, missing the original discovering of history and nature in their different realities.
